title: How companies are valued — earnings, growth, multiples
subtitle: Valuation is not guesswork. It has a logic, a structure, and a vocabulary. Understanding it changes how you read any financial headline.

body.content: Valuation is the process of estimating what a business is worth. There are several methods, but they converge on a single principle: value is a function of what a business earns, how fast those earnings grow, and how certain those earnings are. Multiples are a shorthand for expressing this relationship.

body.label: The P/E ratio explained
body.content: Price-to-earnings ratio (P/E) is the most widely used valuation multiple. A P/E of 20 means investors are paying $20 for every $1 of annual earnings. A high P/E implies high expected growth or low risk. A low P/E implies the opposite — or a market that has mispriced the stock. Neither is inherently good or bad without context.

items: P/E (Price to Earnings): most common. Compares market price to earnings per share. Useful for profitable companies with stable earnings.
EV/EBITDA (Enterprise Value to Earnings Before Interest, Tax, Depreciation, Amortization): capital structure-neutral. Better for comparing companies with different debt levels.
P/S (Price to Sales): used when earnings are negative or volatile. Common in growth companies and early-stage businesses.
P/B (Price to Book): compares market price to accounting book value. More relevant for asset-heavy industries like banking and insurance.

body.content: Multiples are not answers — they are questions. A low P/E might mean a stock is cheap. Or it might mean the market correctly anticipates declining earnings. A high P/E might mean the market is exuberant. Or it might mean the business genuinely merits a premium for its growth prospects and competitive position. The multiple prompts analysis; it does not replace it.
Valuation multiples are a starting point for analysis, not a conclusion. Always ask why a multiple is where it is — and whether the market's implicit assumption about growth and risk is correct.
